text: Robotic lumbar hernia repair with mesh.
Traditionally, due to the location of lumbar hernias, repair occurs via an open technique.
In this video, we will discuss a robotic preperitoneal technique for repair of a lumbar hernia.
The patient is a seventy-eight year old female who presented to clinic with a seven to eight months of right lower back bulge and pain.
Of note, she had suffered from these symptoms before.
In two thousand seven, she had had her right lumbar hernia repaired via an open technique with mesh.
One year later, in two thousand eight, she suffered recurrence and again had an open repair of her lumbar hernia with mesh.
As a part of the diagnostic workup, we obtained a CT abdomen pelvis which showed a right lumbar hernia containing retroperitoneal and perineptic fat.
After discussion and case conference, we decided the best operation for this patient would be a robotic preperitoneal hernia repair with mesh and a fibrin seal.
The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position, with the surgical area prepped and draped in a sterile manner, marking the beginning of the procedure.
The surgeon accesses the abdomen using a Veress needle and pre-insufflates it to a pressure of fifteen millimeters of mercury.
The surgeon inspects the abdomen, observing no visible contraindications for proceeding with the robotic hernia repair.
Under direct visualization, the surgeon places two additional working ports in the right abdomen to facilitate further exploration and dissection.
The da Vinci robotic system is docked, and the camera is inserted through an eight millimeter port located in the mid-abdomen, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
Inside the abdomen, the surgeon identifies a suitable location to initiate retroperitoneal dissection, using the da Vinci robotic system for precise maneuvering.
The surgeon uses a combination of blunt dissection, sharp dissection, and electrocautery to enter the peritoneum at a location near the liver, aiming to visualize the hernia.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection using robotic instruments until the hernia, along with its sac and surrounding retroperitoneal fat, is visually identified.
The previous mesh is visible in the inferior left portion of the screen, positioned posteriorly to the hernia sac.
Using the da Vinci robotic system, the surgeon attempts to dissect around the hernia sac, aiming to reduce its size within the abdominal cavity.
The cursor points to the previous mesh, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on removing the previous mesh to facilitate the reduction of the hernia sac, using visible dissection techniques.
Following the removal of the previous mesh, the surgeon continues dissecting around the hernia sac, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon successfully reduces the contents of the hernia sac into the abdominal cavity, completing the dissection and repositioning process.
The surgeon closes the hernia defect using a running O stratific suture, followed by reinforcement of the closure with interrupted PDS sutures.
The surgeon measures the closed defect to determine the appropriate size for the mesh, ensuring it will adequately cover the area.
The surgeon excises the mesh from the immobilized peritoneum and repositions it on the opposite side of the peritoneum for subsequent removal.
A twelve by twelve piece of mesh with rounded corners is placed onto the surgical field and aligned to ensure appropriate coverage of the hernia defect.
The surgeon fixates the superior lip of the mesh in place using a running Stratafix suture, securing the mesh over the hernia defect.
The surgeon removes the excised mesh from one of the port sites using an endo-catch bag, ensuring safe extraction from the surgical field.
The surgeon places fascial stitches in preparation for later closure of the fascial layer.
The mesh is visibly fixated in place on the superior edge, overlying the primarily closed hernia defect.
The surgeon applies fiber and glue over the mesh to secure its fixation to the abdominal wall, ensuring stable coverage of the hernia defect.
The surgeon closes the flap using a running O stratifix suture, securing the tissue layers together.
The surgeon performs a final inspection of the abdominal cavity, ensuring hemostasis by checking for any remaining bleeding or exposed vessels.
The surgeon removes the laparoscopic ports, closes the fascia, and concludes the procedure.
The patient stayed overnight for monitoring.
On post-op day one, the patient was discharged, tolerating a regular diet and oral pain medications.
The patient was seen in clinic two weeks post-op and was doing well.

The purpose of this video is to present a minimally invasive approach to recurrent lumbar hernia repair, specifically a robotic preperitoneal approach, as a promising alternative to open repair of lumbar hernias.
